request.question: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?

title: Grammatical person
Direct–inverse language
Third Person (band)
Kim Basinger
Du-reformen
Third Person (film)
Pitcairn's Island (novel)
Note verbale
Shinji Mikami
Appears
sentences: Grammatical person, in linguistics, is the grammatical distinction between deictic references to participant(s) in an event; typically the distinction is between the speaker (first person), the addressee (second person), and others (third person).

Put in simple colloquial English, first person is that which includes the speaker, namely, "I," "we," "me," and "us," second person is the person or people to whom are spoken, literally, "you," and third person includes all that is not listed above.
Grammatical person typically defines a language's set of personal pronouns.
It also frequently affects verbs, and sometimes nouns or possessive relationships.

The definition of a direct–inverse language is a matter under research, but it is widely understood to involve different grammar for transitive predications according to the relative positions of their "subject" and their "object" on a "person hierarchy", which, in turn, is some combination of saliency and animacy specific to a given language.
The direct construction is the unmarked one.
The direct construction is used when the subject of the transitive clause outranks the object in the person hierarchy, and the inverse is used when the object outranks the subject.
The existence of direct–inverse morphosyntax is usually accompanied by proximate–obviative morphosyntax.
The direct–inverse dimension subsumes the proximate–obviative dimension.

Across languages, obviation almost always involves the third person (although second-person obviation is reported for some Nilo-Saharan languages ), and the direct–inverse alternation is usually presented as being a way of marking the proximate–obviative distinction between two (or more) third person arguments of a sentence.

However, there are at least two languages with inverse systems, the Mesoamerican languages Zoque and Huastec, in which inverse morphosyntax is never used when both subject and object are third person, but only when one of these arguments is third person and the other is a speech act participant (SAP), the first or second person .
Third Person was an improvising trio formed in 1990 in New York City, led by cellist Tom Cora and drummer Samm Bennett.
Each performance featured an invited guest: A third person.
Guests included guitarist Marc Ribot, harpist Zeena Parkins, clarinet player Don Byron and saxophone players George Cartwright and Kazutoki Umezu and many others.
Kimila Ann "Kim" Basinger ( ; born December 8, 1953) is an American actress, singer and former fashion model.
Following a successful modeling career in New York during the 1970s, Basinger moved to Los Angeles where she began her acting career on television in 1976.
She starred in several made-for-TV films, including a remake of "From Here to Eternity" (1979), before making her feature debut in the 1981 drama "Hard Country".
She won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for her performance as Lynn Bracken in the 1997 film "L.A. Confidential".
Du-reformen (] , "the you-reform") was the process in the late 1960s of popularization of the second-person singular pronoun "du" as a universal form of address in Sweden.

The use of "du" (cognate with English "thou", French "tu", and German "du") replaced an intricate former system where people chiefly addressed each other in third person, with or without a preceding Mr./Mrs./Ms. "(herr, fru "or" fröken)" before the title, often omitting both surname and given name.

Less respectfully, people could be addressed with Mr./Mrs./Ms. plus surname, or in a family setting, alternatively even less respectfully, with the plain name or third person pronoun "han" ("he") or "hon" (she).

Third Person is a 2013 British-German-American romance film directed and written by Paul Haggis and starring an ensemble cast consisting of Liam Neeson, Mila Kunis, Adrien Brody, Olivia Wilde, James Franco, Moran Atias, Kim Basinger, and Maria Bello.
The film premiered at the 2013 Toronto Film Festival.
Pitcairn's Island is the third installment in the fictional trilogy by Charles Nordhoff and James Norman Hall about the mutiny aboard HMS "Bounty".
It is preceded by "Mutiny on the "Bounty"" and "Men Against the Sea".
The novel first appeared in "The Saturday Evening Post" (from 22 September 1934 through 3 November 1934) then was published in 1934 by Little, Brown and Company.
Chapters I–XV are told in the third person, and Chapters XVI–XXI are told in the first person by John Adams.
The epilogue that follows is in the third person.

Note verbale (] , verbal note) is a piece of diplomatic correspondence prepared in the third person and unsigned: less formal than a note (also called a letter of protest) but more formal than an aide-mémoire.
A note verbale can also be referred to as a third person note (TPN).
Shinji Mikami (Japanese: 三上 真司 , Hepburn: Mikami Shinji , born August 11, 1965) is a Japanese video game director and producer.
Starting his career at Capcom in 1990, he went on to direct many of the company's biggest titles.
He directed the first installment of the survival horror series "Resident Evil" in 1996 and the first installment of "Dino Crisis" in 1999.
He returned to Resident Evil to direct the remake of the first game in 2002.
He returned to direct third person shooter "Resident Evil 4" in 2005.
The last game he directed at Capcom was 2006's God Hand, a third person brawler.
He then worked with PlatinumGames to direct the third person shooter "Vanquish" in 2010.
After that he started his own studio called Tango Gameworks.
Under that company he directed the third person horror game "The Evil Within".
The company has since been acquired by the American company ZeniMax Media.
"Appears" is a song recorded by Japanese recording artist Ayumi Hamasaki.
It was released by Avex Trax on November 10, 1999 as the sixth single from her second studio album "Loveppears" (1999), which was released on the same day.
Alongside this, it has been released in several other territories with different release dates under her Western alias Ayu.
It also served as Hamasaki's first limited edition single, limiting physical sales to 300,000 copies.
The track was written by Hamasaki herself, while production was handled by long-time collaborator Max Matsuura.
Musically, "Appears" is a dance song written in third person perspective, and is about the third person watching a happy and loving relationship.
Upon its release, "Appears" received positive reviews from music critics.
Alexey Eremenko, writing for AllMusic, selected the track as the best song from the album and her career.
However, an editor from "CD Journal" criticized the amount of remixes on the CD single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?
hop1_docs: From Here to Eternity (Envy album) | From Here To Eternity is the second album by the band Envy.  It was released in Japan in 1998 by the label H.G. Fact in both CD and LP formats.  As has been noted by reviewers, this album shows a logical progression from the previous one, and indicates to the listener which direction the band will take their sound in the future.

From Here to Eternity: Live | From Here to Eternity: Live is a live album by English punk rock band The Clash.  It was released on 4 October 1999 through Epic Records.  The songs were recorded at different shows.  Some of the recordings featured also appear in the film "Rude Boy".  "London's Burning", "What's My Name" and "I Fought the Law" were instrumentally overdubbed to repair some technical deficiencies of the original live recording.
From Here to Eternity (disambiguation) | From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by songwriter, producer and performer Giorgio Moroder, released in 1977.

From Here to Eternity (miniseries) | From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film "From Here to Eternity" based on the 1951 novel of the same name.  All three conclude with the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor.  The miniseries originally aired in three two-hour installments on three consecutive Wednesdays on NBC on February 14, 21 and 28, 1979.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album) | From Here to Eternity is a 1977 album composed, produced and performed by Italian musician Giorgio Moroder.

From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by English heavy metal band Iron Maiden, released as the second single from their album "Fear of the Dark", released in 1992.
hop1_titles: From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song)
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album)
From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song)
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage states "'From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film.'" and also "'From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries ... originally aired ... on February 14, 1979, February 21, 1979 and February 28, 1979.'" Missing / next-hop: no passage mentions "Third Person" or any cast members. Retrieve the cast list for "Third Person (film)" and the cast list(s) for "From Here to Eternity" (1953 film and the 1979 miniseries) to find any actress appearing in both; use the quoted phrases above as anchors when locating the correct "From Here to Eternity" entry to compare.
response.hop2_query: Third Person (film) cast list "From Here to Eternity" 1953 cast 1979 miniseries cast
hop2_docs: From Here to Eternity (TV series) | From Here to Eternity is a dramatic television series that aired in 1980.  It is a spinoff of the 1979 miniseries of the same title.  The series features most of the cast members from the original miniseries, including William Devane and Kim Basinger.  Barbara Hershey replaced Natalie Wood for the role of Karen Holmes.

From Here to Eternity (miniseries) | From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film "From Here to Eternity" based on the 1951 novel of the same name.  All three conclude with the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor.  The miniseries originally aired in three two-hour installments on three consecutive Wednesdays on NBC on February 14, 21 and 28, 1979.

From Here to Eternity | From Here to Eternity is a 1953 drama film directed by Fred Zinnemann and based on the novel of the same name by James Jones.  The picture deals with the tribulations of three U.S. Army soldiers, played by Burt Lancaster, Montgomery Clift, and Frank Sinatra, stationed on Hawaii in the months leading up to the attack on Pearl Harbor.  Deborah Kerr and Donna Reed portray the women in their lives and the supporting cast includes Ernest Borgnine, Philip Ober, Jack Warden, Mickey Shaughnessy, Claude Akins, and George Reeves.

Forward caste | Forward caste (also known as Forward Class, Forward Community, and General Class) is a term used in India to denote groups of people who do not qualify for any of the affirmative action schemes operated by the government of India.  These schemes are known as Reservation Benefits.  Forward castes form about 20–30 percent of the population, the number varying by region.  Those groups who qualify are listed as Other Backward castes or Scheduled castes and scheduled tribes, and they can take advantage of defined quotas amongst other benefits for education, tax benefits , special government schemes, government employment and political representation.  The lists of Forward, Other Backward and Scheduled castes, and Scheduled tribes are compiled irrespective of religion.  The lists are subject to change from time to time, dependent upon social, educational and economic factors.  However, although lists are produced for those groups entitled to take advantage of the quotas, there is no such list for the Forward caste group; if a person does not belong to any of the listed groups then that person is by default a member of a group classed as Forward caste.

Small castes | Small Castes are not Castes, they are Professions.  After the Indian Rebellion of 1857, the British occupied the whole Indian subcontinent.  They introduced a new policy of divide and rule to sustain their occupation in this region .  According to this policy, society was divided into two parts, i.e. agricultural and non-agricultural castes.  The skilled and educated persons of the community were designated as small castes, or non-agricultural castes.  Their real castes were removed from the records and the profession was avowed as their caste.

The Young and the Restless cast members | "The Young and the Restless" is a long-running American television soap opera, created by William J. Bell and Lee Phillip Bell for CBS.  It first aired on March 26, 1973.  The longest-running current cast member is Doug Davidson, who has portrayed private investigator Paul Williams since May 23, 1978.  Jeanne Cooper, who portrayed the soap opera's matriarch Katherine Chancellor, holds the record for the series' longest-running cast member, airing from November 1973 until her death in May 2013.  Melody Thomas Scott and Eric Braeden, who portray Nikki and Victor Newman, are the second and third longest-running cast members, having joined in February 1979 and February 1980, respectively.  Kate Linder has portrayed Esther Valentine since April 1982, and rounds out the series' top four longest-running cast members.  The following list is of cast members who are currently on the show: both main and recurring members, as well as those who are debuting, departing or returning from the series.
List of Transformers film series cast and characters | List of Transformers film series cast and characters
hop2_titles: The Young and the Restless cast members
List of Transformers film series cast and characters
response.summary_2: From Here to Eternity is both a 1953 film (women in the cast include Deborah Kerr and Donna Reed) and a 1979 three-part miniseries (the miniseries featured Kim Basinger and Barbara Hershey); however, none of the retrieved passages provide the cast list for Third Person, so an overlap of actresses between Third Person and From Here to Eternity cannot be confirmed from these sources.
response.answer: cannot be confirmed from these sources.
